rows.v: Chicken Noodle, Consommé, Chicken & Rice, Egg Drop Soup, Italian Wedding
Light starters — pair with simple breads or let them open a multi-course meal.

A true consommé is the ultimate test of a cook — you clarify a cloudy stock by simmering it with a 'raft' of egg whites and lean meat that traps every impurity, leaving a broth so clear you can read print through it.
Cream of Mushroom, Tomato Soup, Broccoli Cheddar, Beer Cheese, Cream of Chicken, Potato Leek
Rich and filling — a crusty bread or simple salad balances the richness.

A classic cream soup isn't just cream and vegetables — it's built on a velouté, a stock thickened with a flour-and-butter roux, one of the French 'mother sauces' that underpin much of Western cooking.
Butternut Squash, Split Pea, Black Bean, Carrot Ginger, Lentil, Cauliflower
Smooth and often vegetable-forward — a swirl of cream or crunchy topping adds contrast.
A purée soup needs no flour or cream to be thick — the blended starch of beans, peas, or squash does all the work, which is why so many of them are naturally vegan and gluten-free.
Lobster Bisque, Shrimp Bisque, Crab Bisque, She-Crab Soup
Luxurious and seafood-forward — a small portion goes a long way as a starter.

The intense flavor of a real bisque comes from the shells, not the meat — crustacean shells are simmered, then strained (and traditionally ground or thickened with rice), squeezing every last drop of flavor from the lobster or crab.
New England Clam Chowder, Manhattan Clam Chowder, Corn Chowder, Seafood Chowder, Cullen Skink
Hearty and chunky — crusty bread or crackers are the classic accompaniment.
The clam chowder rivalry is real — New England's is creamy and white, Manhattan's is red and tomato-based, and a 1939 Maine bill once tried to make putting tomatoes in chowder illegal.
Gazpacho, Vichyssoise, Borscht, Cucumber Soup
Refreshing warm-weather starters — best served well-chilled with a bright garnish.
Not all soup is hot — gazpacho is blended raw and served cold, and vichyssoise, though it sounds like a hot French classic, was created to be eaten chilled.
Pho, Ramen, Tom Kha Gai, Miso Soup, Wonton Soup, Hot & Sour Soup, Laksa, Mulligatawny
A full meal in a bowl — garnishes (herbs, lime, chili) let diners customize each bowl.
The secret to great miso soup is restraint — you must never boil it once the miso goes in, or the heat destroys the living cultures and delicate aroma of the fermented paste.
French Onion, Minestrone, Bouillabaisse, Gumbo, Pozole, Goulash, Beef Bourguignon
Substantial enough to be the main event — often served with bread, rice, or starch on the side.
French onion soup began as humble peasant food — its transformation into a bistro icon came from topping the bowl with bread and cheese and baking it into a bubbling gratin.
